title: Blood on Polyester
stainChemistry: Blood is a protein stain — hemoglobin, fibrin, albumin. Polyester is a chemically durable synthetic: it tolerates enzymes, 3% hydrogen peroxide, and neutral detergents at room temperature. Heat is the constraint — polyester softens above ~170°F and sets protein into the fiber permanently at any hot-water or dryer temperature. Unlike cotton, polyester is hydrophobic, so water-soluble protein washes out faster than it rinses IN, which is why cold-water flush alone clears a fresh stain.
whyThisWorks: Cold water halts protein setting and flushes soluble hemoglobin out before it can bind. Liquid Protein Spotter (enzymatic digestant) then breaks peptide bonds on residual stain — polyester handles protease enzymes safely. 3% hydrogen peroxide oxidizes residual iron-porphyrin pigment to colorless products. NSD removes all chemistry residue before any heat — critical because polyester is the fiber most vulnerable to heat-setting residual chemistry.
defaultAssumption: Assume the stain is fresh unless the user says otherwise. Polyester is forgiving on fresh blood; heat-set or aged stains escalate faster than cotton.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Spray bottle, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Work from the back of the fabric. Flood cold water and blot hard from underneath — hydraulic pressure pushes soluble hemoglobin out the way it came in. Polyester is hydrophobic, so fresh blood often rinses out almost completely in this step alone. Keep going until the towel comes up clear or pale pink. Cold only — hot water cooks the protein into polyester permanently.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B / protease-based digestant)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply liquid Protein Spotter directly to the stained area. PRENETT B or any protease-based enzymatic digestant is the pro canonical — a clear liquid, not a powder. Tamp gently with a soft bristle brush. Protease hydrolyzes peptide bonds on residual protein. If all you have is a powder enzyme pre-soak, dissolve it fully in cold water before applying — never sprinkle dry powder on fabric.

For any remaining pink or brown shadow: apply 3% hydrogen peroxide drop-by-drop. Polyester tolerates peroxide well, but dyed polyester — especially reactive-dyed activewear — can lose color under prolonged contact. Test an inside seam first if the garment is dark or vivid.
Flush with NSD to remove all enzyme and peroxide residue. Work from the back. Blot — do not rub. Residual chemistry under dryer heat is how polyester gets faintly yellow shadows later.

Rinse under cold running water until the water runs clear. Then launder on the polyester setting (cold to warm max — never hot) with regular detergent. Air-dry or tumble on low — polyester softens above ~170°F.

steps: 1. Cold water flush from the back. Polyester clears fresh blood fast.
2. Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B or protease digestant). Tamp. Dwell 5–10 min.
3. 3% hydrogen peroxide drop-by-drop for any shadow. Test inside seam on dyed polyester.
4. NSD flush — remove all enzyme and peroxide residue before any heat.
5. Cool rinse, cool-to-warm laundry. Never hot water, never hot dryer — polyester softens.
products: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B or equivalent)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold through spotting; cold-to-warm laundry only — never hot
warnings: Never use hot water on polyester with blood — cooks protein into the fiber and softens polyester
Never dry on high heat with residual chemistry — yellow shadows set in
Test 3% H₂O₂ on inside seam on vivid / activewear polyester
Never sprinkle enzyme powder dry — dissolve in cold water first

homeSolutions.instruction: Fresh blood on polyester rinses out faster than on cotton — polyester doesn't soak up water the way natural fibers do. But the clock still matters. You have minutes, not hours. Cold water only. Hot water cooks the protein in AND softens the fiber itself.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never use hot water, a warm dryer, or an iron on a blood stain that's still visible.

homeSolutions.instruction: Hold the fabric with the BACK of the stain under cold running water. Let it flow through the fabric the way it came out of it. Keep going until the water running off runs clear or only faintly pink. On polyester, this step alone often clears a fresh stain completely.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme Pretreat — Dissolve in Cold Water First
homeSolutions.instruction: If any shadow remains, use an enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout). Dissolve powder in cold water per the label — never sprinkle powder on fabric. Apply to the stain, let sit 10 minutes. The enzymes digest residual protein.
A drop of clear Dawn on the spot. Work in gently with a fingertip. Polyester fibers are smooth — don't need hard scrubbing. Rinse cool.

For any pink or brown shadow left: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the spot. Watch closely — lift in under a minute as the peroxide oxidizes residual iron. Rinse cool the moment it's gone. Test an inside seam first if the polyester is dark or neon.
Air-dry is safest. If you must tumble, use the LOW setting only — polyester softens above ~170°F and any residual protein sets permanently. Inspect in daylight before machine-drying.

If the blood's been through a hot wash or dryer, is older than 24–48 hours, or survives two home rounds — take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Blood on polyester, age [fresh / overnight / older], treated at home with cold water, enzyme pretreat, and 3% hydrogen peroxide. No heat applied."

steps: 1. Cold water flush from the back of the fabric. Polyester clears fresh blood fast.
2. Enzyme pretreat dissolved in cold water — never sprinkle powder dry.
3. Drop of Dawn, work in gently, rinse cool.
4. For remaining shadow: 3% hydrogen peroxide, rinse as soon as it lifts. Test inside seam first on dark or vivid polyester.
5. Air dry or tumble LOW only — polyester softens above 170°F.
products: Enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout) — dissolved in cold water
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain has been through a hot wash / dryer, is older than 24–48 hours, or survives two home rounds. Take it to a dry cleaner.
warnings: No hot water — cooks protein AND softens polyester
No high-heat drying — yellow-shadow risk from any residual chemistry
Test 3% hydrogen peroxide on inside seam on dark or neon polyester
Dissolve enzyme powder in cold water first — never apply dry

neverDo: Never use hot water on polyester — softens the fiber + sets protein permanently
Never dry on high heat with any residual chemistry — yellows over time
Never sprinkle enzyme powder dry on fabric — always dissolve in cold water first
fiberSensitivities: Polyester tolerates enzymes, 3% H₂O₂, and neutral detergent at room temperature
Heat-sensitive above ~170°F — never hot laundry, always low dryer
Dyed/vivid polyester can lose color under prolonged peroxide — test inside seam first

materialWarnings: No hot water or high-heat drying — polyester softens + protein sets
Test 3% hydrogen peroxide on inside seam for dark / neon polyester
Always dissolve enzyme powder in cold water first

escalation.when: Heat-set by hot wash or dryer, older than 24–48 hours, or survives two home rounds.
escalation.whatToTell: "Blood on polyester, age [fresh / overnight / older], treated at home with cold water, enzyme pretreat, and 3% hydrogen peroxide. No heat applied."

contextQuestions: Has the garment been through a hot wash, dryer, or iron?
Is the polyester dark / neon / activewear dye?
What's the garment — shirt / carpet / upholstery / other?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Polyester is the fiber most sensitive to heat — any hot-water or hot-dryer exposure on a blood stain escalates to pro. Fresh + no heat is almost always a DIY win.
